figures.gloss: The remote sky-father at the head of the Sumerian pantheon — authority so high it barely acts.
figures.facet: He is the pattern later withdrawn-creator sky-heads across the corpus will repeat: named first in every god-list, invoked least in every myth.

figures.gloss: Babylon's champion who splits the primordial sea-mother Tiāmat and builds heaven and earth from her body.
figures.facet: Here the slaying is the creation — the cosmos is literally made from the monster's corpse.

figures.gloss: Shepherd-consort of Inanna, surrendered to the underworld and released for half the year — wept for across the ancient Near East.
figures.facet: His weeping-cult outlived Sumer and Babylon both, still audible in Ezekiel's condemnation of women 'weeping for Tammuz' at the gate of the Jerusalem temple.

figures.gloss: Queen of heaven who descends through seven gates into death, hangs as a corpse, and returns — love and war in one body.
figures.facet: She fuses sex and war undivided — no separate goddess is needed for either domain, because in her a single force does both.

figures.gloss: Queen of the great below, sister of Inanna, before whom even gods must strip away every emblem of power.
figures.facet: Her law is that even divinity is stripped bare at her gate — Inanna the Queen of Heaven enters her sister's realm and, gate by gate, is reduced to a corpse on a hook.

figures.gloss: Lord of the abzu — the sweet subterranean waters beneath Eridu — and keeper of the me, the ordinances of civilization. He shapes humankind from clay in Atrahasis to relieve the toiling Igigi gods, then, alone among the great gods, warns Atrahasis of Enlil's coming flood by speaking to a reed wall.
figures.facet: He is the trickster inside the pantheon's own government — the wise counselor whose repeated end-runs around Enlil's decrees, the flood-warning chief among them, are what keep humanity alive against the executive's will.
figures.provenance: Atrahasis (OB recension, ed. Lambert & Millard, 1969); the Sumerian 'Enki and the World Order' and 'Enki and Ninhursag'; Samuel Noah Kramer & John Maier, 'Myths of Enki, the Crafty God' (1989).

figures.gloss: 'Lord Wind' of Nippur, holder of the Tablet of Destinies and the divine word that cannot be revoked. He grants and withdraws the kingship of cities, and in Atrahasis it is his irritation at humanity's noise that sends first plague, then drought, then the flood.
figures.facet: For most of Mesopotamian history he is the actual executive of heaven — Anu supplies the rank, Enlil does the ruling — and the myths remember him for the two things a king does worst: silencing the world with punishment, and being outwitted by his own vizier.
figures.provenance: Atrahasis; the Sumerian 'Enlil and Ninlil'; Thorkild Jacobsen, 'The Treasures of Darkness' (1976).

figures.gloss: The sun who rises each day through the eastern mountain gates and crosses the sky to judge gods and mortals alike, protector of travelers, merchants and the wronged, and the god shown on Hammurabi's stele handing the king the rod and ring of just rule.
figures.facet: He is the one power in the pantheon whose authority is entirely procedural — not the strongest god, but the one whose verdict nothing else in heaven or earth can overturn.
figures.provenance: The Hymn to Shamash; the relief atop the Code of Hammurabi (Louvre, Sb 8); the Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablets III–V, where he aids Gilgamesh against Humbaba.

figures.gloss: The moon god of Ur, 'lamp of heaven,' whose waxing and waning reckons the months and whose high-priestess office — first held by Sargon's daughter Enheduanna — anchored royal legitimacy at the city's great ziggurat.
figures.facet: His cult outlives Sumer, Babylon and even cuneiform: at Harran it becomes the last living Mesopotamian state religion, still resisting Islam nearly two millennia after Ur's fall.
figures.provenance: Enheduanna's temple hymns (the colophon of 'The Exaltation of Inanna' names her Nanna's priestess); the Sumerian 'Nanna's Journey to Nippur'; on the Harranian survival, Tamara Green, 'The City of the Moon God' (1992).

figures.gloss: Two-thirds-divine king of Uruk whose grief at Enkidu's death drives him past the scorpion-guarded mountain and across the waters of death to Utnapishtim, the flood's survivor, in search of the immortality he ultimately fails to keep.
figures.facet: The epic's real ending is not the failure to find eternal life but the fallback he settles for: the wall of Uruk he built, which will outlast him — meaning made from a city rather than an escape from death.
figures.provenance: The Epic of Gilgamesh, Standard Babylonian Version (ed. Andrew George, 2003); earlier Sumerian Gilgamesh poems.

figures.gloss: The primordial salt sea and mother of the first gods, who — enraged at their clamor and at the death of her consort Apsu — arms herself with eleven monsters and her new champion Kingu, only to be confronted and split in two by Marduk, her body becoming the vault of sky and the deep of earth.
figures.facet: She is Mesopotamia's clearest statement that creation is not summoned from nothing but carved out of something already alive — the ordered world is quite literally a slain mother's corpse.
figures.provenance: Enūma Eliš, Tablets I–IV; W.G. Lambert, 'Babylonian Creation Myths' (2013).

figures.gloss: God of plague, war and the sun's scorching noon who, insulted at a banquet of the gods, storms down to the underworld, seizes Ereshkigal by the hair and threatens to behead her — then relents, marries her, and stays as king beside her.
figures.facet: His myth is the one Mesopotamian story that turns the underworld's queen from sole absolute sovereign into a married co-regent — power below the earth renegotiated first by force, then by treaty.
figures.provenance: 'Nergal and Ereshkigal' (Amarna and Sultantepe versions); Benjamin Foster, 'Before the Muses' (2005).

figures.gloss: 'Lady of the Mountain,' the birth-goddess who helps Enki shape humankind from clay in Atrahasis and who, in the Dilmun cycle, bears the healing deities born of Enki's own transgressions — including Ninti, 'lady of the rib,' who cures his aching rib.
figures.facet: She is the tetrad's working obstetrician: the senior gods decree and desire, but it is Ninhursag's hands that pinch the clay and pull the healing goddesses' bodies into the world.
figures.provenance: Atrahasis; 'Enki and Ninhursag' (ETCSL 1.1.1); Samuel Noah Kramer, 'Sumerian Mythology' (1944).

figures.gloss: The wise man of Shuruppak, warned by Enki through a reed wall to build a boat before Enlil's flood, who rides out the deluge with his kin and the seed of every living thing and is granted immortality at 'the mouth of the rivers' — the one being Gilgamesh crosses the waters of death to consult.
figures.facet: He is proof, inside his own story, that the immortality Gilgamesh wants cannot be re-earned — it was a one-time exception granted for surviving an unrepeatable catastrophe, not a status open to further heroes.
figures.provenance: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet XI; Atrahasis (the fuller, earlier flood account under that name); the Sumerian Flood Story naming Ziusudra.

figures.gloss: The divine ordinances or powers of civilization — kingship, priesthood, crafts, lamentation, scribeship, even the arts of sexual love and deceit — held by Enki at Eridu, possessable as if they were physical objects, and famously gotten drunk out of him by Inanna, who loads them onto her Boat of Heaven and carries them to Uruk.
figures.facet: Civilization itself is imagined here not as an achievement but as a finite set of transferable objects — which means it can be stolen, hoarded in one city, and won away to another.

figures.gloss: The Babylonian creation epic in seven tablets: Apsu's murder, Tiamat's rage and her army of monsters, Marduk's election as champion in exchange for supremacy, his splitting of her corpse into sky and earth, the building of Babylon and Esagila, and the recitation of his fifty names.
figures.facet: It is theology written as regime-change propaganda: an older Sumerian pantheon is not abolished but reorganized, on paper, around one city's god.
figures.provenance: Enūma Eliš, standard edition W.G. Lambert (2013); its liturgical recitation at the Akitu festival is documented in the ritual tablets, per Marc Linssen, 'The Cults of Uruk and Babylon' (2004).

figures.gloss: Babylon's New Year festival: Marduk's cult statue and those of visiting gods process between Esagila and the Akitu-house outside the walls, Enūma Eliš is recited in full, and the king — stripped of his regalia — is slapped by the high priest until he weeps, the tears read as a good omen for the coming year.
figures.facet: For eleven days the cosmic order Enūma Eliš describes is not just remembered but re-run, with the living king standing in for both defeated Tiamat's chaos and Marduk's restored kingship in a single body.
figures.provenance: Akitu ritual tablets (Seleucid-era copies of older originals); Julye Bidmead, 'The Akitu Festival' (2002); Linssen (2004).

figures.gloss: The god Tiamat elevates to general of her monstrous army after Apsu's death, married to her and given the Tablet of Destinies to wear at his breast; defeated with her by Marduk, his blood is mixed with clay by Ea to shape humankind.
figures.facet: He is demoted twice over — from consort-general bearing the highest emblem of cosmic authority to the raw material of the species created to serve the gods who killed him.
figures.provenance: Enūma Eliš, Tablets I, IV, and VI; Lambert, 'Babylonian Creation Myths' (2013).

figures.gloss: Son of Enlil, warrior and farmer god of Nippur who pursues the bird-monster Anzu into the mountains to recover his father's stolen Tablet of Destinies, and in the Sumerian 'Lugal-e' subdues the demon Asag and the rebellious stones, piling their bodies into mountains and ordering the rivers for irrigation.
figures.facet: His combat myths are a full generation older than Marduk's — the plough-and-mace god who first proved that killing a chaos-monster could reorganize the physical landscape, a template Babylon's theologians later hand to Marduk.
figures.provenance: The Anzu Epic (Standard Babylonian version, ed. Amar Annus, 2001); 'Lugal-e' (ETCSL 1.6.2).

figures.gloss: The lion-headed eagle who, admitted into Enlil's sanctuary, steals the Tablet of Destinies while Enlil bathes, seizing the power to fix all fates until Ninurta hunts him down in the mountains and kills him.
figures.facet: Unlike Tiamat's monster-army, born to threaten from outside, Anzu is a trusted palace guard who commits the theft from within — Mesopotamia's rarer myth of treachery at the very seat of power.
figures.provenance: The Anzu Epic (Old Babylonian and Standard Babylonian versions, ed. Amar Annus, 2001).

figures.gloss: God of writing, wisdom and the scribal arts, keeper of the Tablet of Destinies in first-millennium theology that makes him son of Marduk; his cult center at Borsippa sent his cult barge to Babylon each New Year to join his father in the Akitu procession.
figures.facet: As Babylon's power passes from Marduk to his son in temple ideology, the empire quietly re-centers its highest reverence on the god of the written word — the pen inherits what the sword first won.
figures.provenance: Akitu ritual texts naming Nabu's procession from Borsippa; Michael Jursa on the rise of Nabu's cult in the first millennium BCE; the theophoric element 'Nabû-' in royal names (Nabopolassar, Nebuchadnezzar).

edges.note: Independent Chaoskampf: order wrung from the serpent — once at creation (Tiāmat), nightly forever (Apophis).
The dragon-slaying storm-god who becomes the supreme ordering God of his city, then his world.
Descent of a goddess into death and her cyclic return — the season written as a journey below.
The mourned god given to the underworld and released in part — wept for from the Nile to Sumer.
An/Anu, the Mesopotamian sky-head, likewise recedes into remote authority while younger gods (Enlil, Marduk) run the world — a parallel deus-otiosus drift.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Dragon-slaying solar champions who reorganize cosmic and cultural intelligence for a new epoch of human civilization.

The Arabian morning-star goddess belongs to the same Near-Eastern Venus complex as Inanna/Ishtar and Astarte — feared, blood-receiving, identified with the planet; real cultic kinship across Semitic worlds, no direct descent claimed.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The proxy figure who must be ritually slain to ensure agricultural and cosmic renewal.
GOLD SEAM: Male forces of fertility and atmospheric renewal whose presence requires the ardent, formalized courtship of a female counterpart.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The fierce, sovereign goddess who commands wild beasts and whose overwhelming love condemns her shepherd consort to doom.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The dying shepherd consort of the great goddess whose tragic death initiates a ritual cycle of communal weeping.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The perilous descent into an underworld or labyrinth that results in a transformative, triumphant re-emergence as an elevated Queen.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: In the Babylonian Enuma Elish, it is Marduk who explicitly creates the zodiacal constellations to map the year.
GOLD SEAM: Kiririsha and Pinikir were explicitly equated with the Mesopotamian Ishtar/Inanna in cross-cultural treaties.
GOLD SEAM: Humban was formally equated with the Mesopotamian sky-father Anu in ancient god lists.
GOLD SEAM: Both rule the dark, inescapable underworld beneath the Mesopotamian-Elamite cultural sphere.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereign goddesses who undergo a traumatic descent into the underworld, confronting death directly to establish a new cosmic balance.
GOLD SEAM: The foundational dying figure whose tragic descent into death becomes the focal point of a recurring initiatory or seasonal drama.
Both are great goddesses who DESCEND and are restored, but Inanna descends in power to seize the underworld while Sophia falls by error into matter — descent as conquest versus descent as fault.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Inanna's descent into the underworld is actively utilized by traditions like Reclaiming as the foundational myth of shadow-work and feminine initiation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are classic pastoral dying-and-rising consorts whose vitality and sacrificial descent are bound to the agricultural fertility of the sovereign Goddess.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Astaroth is the demonized, gender-flipped derivation of the goddess Astarte, cognate with Inanna.
GOLD SEAM: Heavily adorned, highly fecund divine female figures wielding immense territorial, domestic, and reproductive authority.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Zuhara represents the unbroken, direct continuation of the Ishtar/Inanna planetary cult into the Islamic era.

The god of growth who departs and is awaited — Lono who goes over the sea and returns with the winter rains of the Makahiki, Dumuzi the shepherd who dies into the underworld and rises with the season; the vegetation-deity's annual leaving and return.
Vegetation-gods whose absence withers the land; Anatolia's distinctive twist is that Telipinu is not killed or taken to the underworld but simply hides in anger, then is reconciled.
The Anatolian Anu of the Kumarbi cycle is the Mesopotamian sky-god An/Anu adopted into Hurrian-Hittite myth — a documented borrowing of the heaven-king into the succession story.

Both belong to the Near-Eastern generational-combat myth-world (Enuma Elish's Tiamat-slaying, the Kumarbi cycle's succession), sharing the theme of a new divine order won by violence against an elder power.

The stone giant grown from the sea against the gods rhymes with Tiamat's brood of monsters in the Babylonian combat-myth — the elder power's monstrous champion confronted by the storm-champion of the new order.

A tempting but unproven link: Inara's name has been compared to Inanna/Ishtar, and both are fierce goddesses of the wild and of stratagem — but the etymological connection is contested, so flagged speculative.
GOLD SEAM: Shaushka was explicitly identified with Ishtar/Inanna, mirroring her exact spheres of sexual seduction and martial violence.
Both turn on a descent to a watery/underworld realm to restore the flow of life; Sedna's undersea house and Inanna's underworld each gate the world's fertility behind a journey down.

Wounded, withholding queen of the deep whose grievance must be soothed before she releases what she holds — Ereshkigal's combed hair in the Inanna myth uncannily rhymes with the angakkuq combing Sedna's.
An/Anu likewise drifts into remote authority while younger powers run the world — a parallel withdrawn-creator across families.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The female figure who is stripped of all garments and societal regalia to enter the absolute truth of the divine realm.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent fierce, autonomous expressions of the divine feminine who traverse the underworld and refuse subjugation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are dread sovereigns of the underworld who administer strict, uncompromising justice and cannot be defied.
GOLD SEAM: Both are demiurgic heroes who slay chaotic monstrosities and architect the physical universe from their butchered corpses.

Chaoskampf across families: Kai-Kai is the watery-chaos serpent like Tiamat — but where Marduk SLAYS Tiamat and builds the world from her body, Kai-Kai is answered by a rival serpent who out-lifts her, the combat resolved by raising land, not by butchery. (Tiamat herself is not yet in the mirror; seamed to her slayer.)
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Rulers of a dangerous, trap-laden underworld who strip descending heroes or deities of their power and vitality.
GOLD SEAM: Youthful male consorts intimately bound to the agricultural cycle, destined to die and descend to the underworld while the great goddess remains supreme.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereign female deities flanked by predatory felines, commanding absolute terrestrial and celestial authority without reliance on a male king.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereign goddesses of supreme authority whose cults are entirely integrated into massive, centralized bureaucratic redistributive economies.

Champions who slay the primordial monster of chaos and from its body shape the ordered world — Marduk splits Tiamat, Monster Slayer's giant-blood becomes the lava beds; world-from-the-slain-foe, independently reached.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The dying and rising god whose life cycle is violently and intimately bound to the sprouting and harvesting of staple crops.
Sovereign of a realm below — the undersea house as analogue to the underworld throne, master of what the living cannot reach.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Fierce, absolute queens of the underworld who preside over the dead and demand strict appeasement.

Two queens of the night-world who receive all the dead into the dark; Hine-nui-te-pō diverges as the wronged first-daughter who went down by her own shame, the gentle gatherer rather than the dread Mesopotamian sovereign of the great below.
GOLD SEAM: Supreme, astral Queens of Heaven who eclipsed their male counterparts to become the fiercely protective patrons of vast urban empires.
GOLD SEAM: Dying-and-rising gods whose annual awakening ('egersis') ensures the renewal of the earthly and agricultural order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A fiercely independent queen of desire and underworld power, weaponizing sexuality to subordinate male authority.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereign goddesses of heaven and fertility whose cults profoundly center on ecstatic, gender-transgressing priesthoods.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme champion invested with the combined weapons of the elder gods to slay the chaos-monster and re-establish the world's structure.
GOLD SEAM: An ancient, formidable goddess of life and death whose cult was eventually systematized into a patriarchal pantheon, yet who retained independent sovereign power.
GOLD SEAM: The distant, ultimate sky father who presides over the divine assembly and delegates the actual running of the cosmos to a hierarchy of lesser deities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent sovereign, formidable, and untamed aspects of the feminine divine dwelling in the underworld, entirely autonomous from patriarchal deities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The mythological roots of Lilith trace back directly to the Lilitu demons associated with Inanna in ancient Mesopotamian religion.
GOLD SEAM: Both are fiercely independent, sexually liberated divine females who master the domains of both heaven and the underworld.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereign goddesses whose wild, ecstatic rites deliberately dissolve social norms, employing deafening music and gender transgression.
GOLD SEAM: UFO mythologies often explicitly syncretize ancient Mesopotamian sky gods like Anu as historical misidentifications of visiting extraterrestrials.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A fiercely independent queen of both divine desire and underworld power, weaponizing sexuality and refusing patriarchal subordination.
GOLD SEAM: A localized city-god elevated to the absolute head of an imperial pantheon through political conquest.
GOLD SEAM: Complex, tempestuous spirits encompassing the terrifying extremes of both boundless desire and devastating wrath.
GOLD SEAM: Both embody the sacrificial shepherd-consort whose cyclical death ensures the ongoing fertility of the earth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The formidable, fearsome goddess who commands the realm of the dead and dictates the fate of mortals and elites alike.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The vegetative, agricultural spirit whose cyclical life is bound to the earth's fertility and necessitates continuous propitiation.
A faint structural touch: both concern the entry of death into the human order, but Dumuzi's is a seasonal dying-rising, the chameleon's a once-for-all miscarried message. Flagged as a stretch.
Enthroned queens of a subterranean land of the dead, ruling not as consorts alone but as sovereigns of a morally neutral underworld.
inexorable underworld sovereigns to whose realm a fertility/storm god (Baal / by structural parallel Dumuzi-Inanna's cycle) must descend before the cosmos's vitality can resume.
fierce Bronze-Age war goddesses of blood-soaked battle whose cults overlapped and were sometimes conflated in the ancient Near East.
Treaty god-lists equate Pinikir directly with Ishtar/Inanna, alongside the corpus's existing Kiririsha-Inanna seam.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: independent goddesses of love and vital force, each iconographically central to her tradition's visual and cult repertoire — Turan on the Etruscan mirror, Inanna/Ishtar on Mesopotamian glyptic and hymn.

Aphrodite's cult is traced by Herodotus and modern scholarship to Phoenician Astarte, herself a western avatar of Inanna/Ishtar — a documented but indirect line, weakened here to analogy since Astarte, the actual intermediary, is not itself a corpus node.

Underworld sovereigns of the same Ancient Near Eastern religious milieu — both rule the realm of the dead and are petitioned by royal courts on the king's behalf, in regions with documented cultural contact.
the Hurrian Anu is the borrowed Mesopotamian sky-god An/Anu under his Hurrian succession-myth role — a direct name-and-cult borrowing across the Sumerian-Akkadian-Hurrian-Hittite continuum.
both are sovereign queens of the underworld who receive the deposed and the dead into a structured court rather than a formless pit.

The name is borrowed from the actual Mesopotamian divine collective (the Anunnaki, 'those of princely seed,' offspring of An/Anu) and recast as extraterrestrial invaders — a modern ancient-astronaut-style reinterpretation with no support in Assyriology; the fabricated version set beside the real name it borrows.
Co-shapers of humankind from clay in Atrahasis, and partners again in the Dilmun cycle where his own transgressions become her cure.
He is their keeper at Eridu until Inanna gets him drunk enough to give them all away.
She loads the stolen ordinances of civilization onto the Boat of Heaven and carries them from Eridu to Uruk.
The hero crosses the waters of death expressly to question the one man who was granted what he seeks.
Warned through a reed wall by the god who defies Enlil's decree of silence, he alone survives to found the flood story's second humanity.
The flood-sender confronted, after the fact, by the very existence of the man his own decree was meant to erase.
Split down the middle to build sky and earth from her corpse — the founding act Enūma Eliš exists to justify.
Consort and general, elevated together, defeated together; his blood and her armistice both become raw material for the new order.
Two competing Mesopotamian anthropogonies mix clay with the blood of a slain god to make humankind — Enūma Eliš uses Kingu's, Atrahasis (Ninhursag's version) uses the executed god Geshtu-e's.
The storm-champion of Nippur pursues the bird-monster into the mountains to recover his father's stolen Tablet of Destinies.
Son avenges father: Ninurta's combat with Anzu restores the emblem of Enlil's own authority.
The theft is committed inside Enlil's own sanctuary while he bathes — treachery at the seat of power, not an outside assault.
The Ninurta combat myths (Anzu Epic, Lugal-e) supply the type-scene Enūma Eliš's Marduk-Tiamat combat reworks into imperial theology a millennium later.
Son and father in first-millennium Babylonian theology, paired in the Akitu procession from Borsippa to Babylon.
His cult barge processes from Borsippa to join Marduk's at the New Year rite.
The creation epic is recited in full during the festival, liturgically tying text to rite.
Father and daughter: his moon cult at Ur anchors the family whose daughter becomes Uruk's queen of heaven.
Father and son: Nanna/Sin of Ur begets Utu/Shamash, the sun who judges by day what his father the moon reckons by month.
Twin siblings, children of Nanna/Sin — sun and Venus, justice and desire, born of the same moon.
The same moon god and cult, continuing under the same name at Harran for nearly two millennia after Ur's fall, into the Islamic era.
Nahhunte, the Elamite sun god of justice, is the direct regional counterpart of Utu/Shamash, in the same pattern as the corpus's other documented Mesopotamian-Elamite god-list equations.
The fourth member of the senior tetrad — An, Enlil, Enki, Ninhursag — Sumerian theology names as the gods who 'decree the fates.'
independently invented primordial mountain/earth mothers from whose body life-giving and healing deities are born.
the resident cunning-and-wisdom god of each pantheon, patron of incantation and hidden knowledge, whose cleverness resolves crises the more powerful gods cannot.
goddesses who unite erotic love and battle-death in a single figure — Freyja both the foremost beauty of the North and receiver of half the slain, Inanna/Ishtar goddess equally of love and war.

the storm-champion against the sea-serpent of chaos — though Marduk's slaying of Tiamat founds the ordered world, while Jörmungandr's mutual death with Þórr accompanies the world's ending, the same chaoskampf shape inverted.
astral goddesses of love and war whose cult titles and iconography (the Queen of Heaven complex) traveled across the whole Semitic Near East.
chaoskampf structural parallel — the champion whose combat against monstrous adversaries defends or founds cosmic order.

Both are water/fertility goddesses bound up with royal power and warfare; Berossus records the Achaemenid court under Artaxerxes II explicitly linking Anahita's new cult statues to Ishtar's, though the identification was a political program, not a native Iranian one.

The Hurrian-Hittite Kingship in Heaven cycle imports the Mesopotamian sky-king's name unchanged as Anu, then gives him a fall — castrated and dethroned by Kumarbi — that the Mesopotamian original never suffers.

dossier.essence: The polytheistic religion of ancient Mesopotamia — Sumer, Akkad, Babylonia, and Assyria — in the Fertile Crescent, from the rise of the first cities and cuneiform writing (c. 3500 BCE) to the last datable cuneiform tablets around 80 CE. It was a civic, temple-centered religion of many gods, each the patron of a city, in which humanity was created to labor on the gods' behalf. Its literature — Gilgamesh, the Enuma Elish, the Descent of Inanna — is among the oldest recorded religious thought.
dossier.cosmos: The cosmos arose when the gods separated heaven and earth from a primeval watery chaos; in Babylon's Enuma Elish, Marduk slays the salt-sea dragon Tiamat and builds the world from her corpse, then makes humankind from clay and the blood of a slain god to relieve the gods of toil. The great powers include An/Anu (sky), Enlil (air and kingship), Enki/Ea (wisdom and fresh water), the sun-judge Utu/Shamash, and Inanna/Ishtar of love and war. The dead descend to a gloomy 'land of no return' (Irkalla) ruled by Ereshkigal, a dusty realm without reward or punishment, where they depend on offerings from the living.
dossier.practice: Worship centered on the temple, often crowned by a ziggurat, where the god dwelt in a cult statue that was daily fed, washed, and clothed by a professional priesthood, with the king as the god's earthly steward. The ritual year climaxed in festivals such as Babylon's Akitu (New Year), when the Enuma Elish was recited and kingship renewed. Divination — extispicy (liver-reading) and celestial omens — and incantation against demons were pervasive.
dossier.canon: No single scripture: a vast cuneiform literature on clay, including the Epic of Gilgamesh, the Enuma Elish, the Descent of Inanna/Ishtar, Atrahasis, temple hymns, and the great omen and incantation series (e.g., Enuma Anu Enlil, Maqlû).
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds humanity as the gods' servant-labor — religion as the upkeep of a divine household through offering and service, under a bleak, morally undifferentiated afterlife that placed all hope in this-worldly favor.
sources: Thorkild Jacobsen, 'The Treasures of Darkness: A History of Mesopotamian Religion' (1976)
Benjamin R. Foster, 'Before the Muses: An Anthology of Akkadian Literature' (3rd ed., 2005)
